coredata.dc:description: Given the photocatalytic properties of semiconducting polymers and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), we report a new structure for a metal-free photocatalytic system with a promising efficiency for hydrogen production through the combination of an organic semiconducting polymer (PFTBTA) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) covered by PS-PEGCOOH to produce heterostructured photocatalysts in the form of polymer dots (Pdots). This design could provide strong interactions between the two materials owing to the space confinement effect in nanometer-sized Pdots. Small particle size NCQDs are easy to insert inside the Pdot, which leads to an increase in the stability of the Pdot structure and enhances the hydrogen evolution rate by approximately 5-fold over that of pure PFTBTA Pdots. The photophysics and the mechanism behind the catalytic activity of our design are investigated by transient absorption measurement, demonstrating the role of NCQDs to enhance the charge separation and the photocatalytic efficiency of the PFTBTA Pdot.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure and design concept of the NCQD/PFTBTA Pdots for visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution.
The schematic diagram for the preparation of PFTBTA Pdot, wNCQDs/PS-PEGCOOH and wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot.
(a and b) TEM (top)/DLS (down) of wNCQDs and sNCQDs; (c) Raman spectra of wNCQDs and sNCQDs; (d) XRD patterns of wNCQDs and sNCQDs.

Contact angles of the PFTBTA polymer, wNCQDs, sNCQDs, PFTBTA Pdots, wNCQD/PS-PEGCOOH and wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots (top). TEM (middle) images and hydrodynamic diameter (down) spectra of PFTBTA Pdots, wNCQD/PS-PEGCOOH and wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots.

(a) XPS of the wNCQDs, PFTBTA Pdots and wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots, (b) high-resolution of the C 1s peak in wNCQDs. (c) high-resolution of the C 1s peak in PFTBTA Pdots and wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots, (d) high-resolution of the N 1s peak in wNCQDs and wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots.
a) Absorption (dotted line) and emission (solid line) spectra of the PFTBTA Pdots and wNCQDs-PFTBTA Pdot in water. b) Absorption of wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot at different concentrations of wNCQDs.

a) Time course of the produced H2 for the PFTBTA Pdots, wNCQDs/PS-PEGCOOH and wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdots and b) PFTBTA Pdots, sNCQDs/PS-PEGCOOH, and sNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdots. c) Time course of the produced H2 for the PFTBTA Pdots and 1 % wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots for 10 and 21 h, respectively, d) UV–vis spectrum of 1 % wNCQD-PFTBTA Pdots and PFTBTA Pdots before and after the hydrogen evolution experiments. Conditions: Diethylamine (DEA, 2 mL), white LED light (λ > 420 nm, 20 W, 6500 K).

a) Control experiments of the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots. b) Time-resolved fluorescence decay of IRF, PFTBTA Pdots, and 1 % wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots. c) Transient photocurrent measurements of the corresponding samples, d) Photoluminescence emission spectra of various photocatalysts.

(a)TA spectra of wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot (water solution) at different delay times (b) TA spectra of PFTBTA Pdot (water solution) at different delay times (c) TA spectra of wNCQDs (water solution) at different delay times (d) TA traces for PFTBTA Pdot with (Greenline) and without (Redline) wNCQDs. The data were obtained using an excitation wavelength of 450 nm and a power of 10 μW.
Proposed photocatalytic reaction mechanism of PFTBTA Pdots with and without wNCQDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Newly designed heterostructured polymer dots photocatalyst consists of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) implanted PFTBTA Pdot covered by PS-PEGCOOH was first demonstrated, which enhance the HER and photocatalytic stability compare to that of pure PFTBTA Pdots under otherwise identical conditions.

Given the photocatalytic properties of semiconducting polymers and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), we report a new structure for a metal-free photocatalytic system with a promising efficiency for hydrogen production through the combination of an organic semiconducting polymer (PFTBTA) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) covered by PS-PEGCOOH to produce heterostructured photocatalysts in the form of polymer dots (Pdots). This design could provide strong interactions between the two materials owing to the space confinement effect in nanometer-sized Pdots. Small particle size NCQDs are easy to insert inside the Pdot, which leads to an increase in the stability of the Pdot structure and enhances the hydrogen evolution rate by approximately 5-fold over that of pure PFTBTA Pdots. The photophysics and the mechanism behind the catalytic activity of our design are investigated by transient absorption measurement, demonstrating the role of NCQDs to enhance the charge separation and the photocatalytic efficiency of the PFTBTA Pdot.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) is one of the most important and ideal energy carriers for many reasons, such as its bountiful supply and efficient, clean and non-toxic energy sources, and H2O is the only oxidation product of H2 [1]. Using solar energy for H2 production from water is a significant and extraordinary strategy that has been extensively studied with great interest after the first report by Fujishima and Honda on the photoelectrochemical splitting of water using an n-type TiO2 electrode [2]. Developing environmentally friendly, low-cost, and highly active visible-light-driven photocatalysts that are stable in aqueous conditions should lead to large-scale applications of H2 generation [3]. Numerous metal-based inorganic semiconductors have been used more widely for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution because of their long lifetime and high energy-conversion efficiency [4]; however, these materials suffer from some common restrictions, including wide bandgaps, conduction band potentials lower than the reduction potential of protons, toxicity, instability and/or high cost [5]. In contrast, metal-free organic photocatalysts, including polymers [6], carbon-based materials, such as graphitic carbon nitride [7–9], carbon quantum dots [10] and graphene oxide [11]; boron materials (boron carbide or boron nitride) [12,13] and black phosphorus [14] have more advantages, including cost-effectiveness, low toxicity, facile tuneable bandgaps and their capability for diverse molecular modifications.

Recently, many organic semiconducting polymers, including conjugated linear polymers [15–22], conjugated microporous polymers [23], covalent triazine frameworks and organic frameworks [24], have shown good potential as photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution. However, most organic semiconducting polymers are hydrophobic materials, which greatly limits their dispersibility in water. Therefore, the limited interaction between the photocatalyst and water molecules during the hydrogen evolution rate (HER) might inhibit the efficiency of these photocatalysts. Therefore, a certain amount of organic solvent (methanol) is required to increase their dispersibility in the reaction phase. Recently, polymer dots (Pdots) have been shown to be highly efficient photocatalysts that can improve dispersibility in water without using organic solvents were reported by Tian; however, the long-term stability of Pdots is still a concern [25,26]. Recently, Chen and co-workers reported polyfluorene Pdots that were facilely coupled with g-C3N4 nanosheets to form organic heterojunctions to enhance the HER and photocatalytic function [27,28]. Similarly, Tian and his group reported hollow structure Pdots as efficient photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution [29]. Hu and coworkers reported Pdots of hyperbranched soluble conjugated polymer nanoparticles (SCPNs) as photocatalysts for the HER without adding a noble metal cocatalyst [30]. Very recently, we developed the first cycloplatinated Pdots in which the HER and stability of the Pdots were dramatically enhanced by our design [20,31]. Although the incorporation of Pt can efficiently enhance the photocatalytic reaction, the development of low-cost and metal-free photocatalysts is still a promising design strategy.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical particles with a multilayer graphene structure and the advantages of low toxicity, air stability, natural abundance, inexpensive nature [32], stable photoluminescence, and electron reservoir properties. In addition, they are widely applied in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [33] and for the detection of ions [34], bioimaging [35], drug delivery, and sensors. CQDs have attracted increasing interest because of their novel applications in energy conversion [36]. In addition, the use of N-doped CQDs (NCQDs) is an effective approach to enhance the electronic density and tune the optical and electrical properties. Previous reports have used an inorganic semiconductor to enhance the photocatalytic activity of hybrid materials with CQDs [37,38]. However, hybrid materials of NCQDs and conjugated polymers have never been studied as photocatalysts for the HER. In addition, due to CQDs previously being physically blended with photocatalysts in a solution system, the interactions between each other are quite weak. Unlike inorganic photocatalysts, both the structure of the conjugated polymer and NCQDs is constructed by aromatic units, which can provide stronger π-π intermolecular interactions between each other to facilitate electron transfer during a photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, if we can implant the NCQDs into Pdots, we believe that the materials will have even stronger interactions due to the space confinement effect in nanometer-sized Pdots. As a result, the NCQDs can play a more efficient role in altering electron transfer during the HER. Herein, we prepared NCQDs that were facilely implanted with conjugated polymers inside Pdots to form organic heterojunctions to enhance the HER with low HER roll-offs and long-term photocatalytic function (Fig. 1 ).

Notably, to form the NCQD-implanted Pdots, NCQDs with hydrophobic properties are necessary to facilitate the insertion of NCQDs into the Pdot structure. The introduction of NCQDs within the Pdots will enhance the hydrogen evolution performance without using expensive and highly toxic noble metals in the solution system or an organic solvent to increase the dispersibility of the polymer in the reaction phase. More importantly, both polymers and NCQDs have excellent bandgap alignment and synergic cooperation, and we are interested in studying the effect of different particle sizes and different bandgaps of NCQDs with polymers on the photocatalytic performance. Therefore, we prepared two different particle sizes of NCQDs with different bandgaps: first, small particle size NCQDs (approximately 4 nm) with a wide bandgap; this type was successful in entering the Pdots to form wide-bandgap NCQD (wNCQD)-implanted Pdots, and the energy levels of both the wNCQDs and PFTBTA are compatible for charge separation. As a result, inserting the wNCQDs inside the Pdots leads to an increase in the stability of the Pdot structure and enhances the hydrogen evolution rate by approximately 5-fold over that of pure PFTBTA Pdots. In contrast, the large particle size NCQDs (approximately 18 nm), which have a small bandgap and are referred to as sNCQDs, have energy levels that are not compatible with those of PFTBTA. Therefore, optimizing the size and ratio of NCQDs allowed us to enhance the photocatalytic performance of the NCQD-implanted Pdots as photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution under visible-light-driven and metal-free conditions.

All reagents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. Citric acid (CA), diaminonapthalene (DAN), and ascorbic acid were purchased from Acros. Tetrakis(triphenylphosphine)palladium(0) (Pd(PPh3)4), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), tetra-n-butylammonium bromide (TBAB), bromobenzene, phenylboronic acid, diethylamine (DEA), and triethylamine (TEA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. 2,2′-(9,9-dioctyl-9H-fluorene-2,7-diyl)bis(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane) (F-B), and 4,7-bis(5-bromothiophen-2-yl)-5,6-difluoro-2-octyl-2H-benzo[d][1,2,3]triazole (TBTA-Br), were synthesized following previous literature procedures [39].

The PFTBTA polymer prepared as shown in Scheme S1 by Suzuki–Miyaura coupling polymerization [31]. Toluene (40 mL) and water (10 mL) were injected into a sealed tube charged with the first monomer F-B (2,2′-(9,9-dioctyl-9H-fluorene-2,7-diyl)bis(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane) (643 mg 1.0 mmol), the second monomer TBTA-Br (4,7-bis(5-bromothiophen-2-yl)-5,6-difluoro-2-octyl-2H-benzo[d][1,2,3]triazole) (589 mg, 1.0 mmol), K2CO3 (2544 mg, 24.0 mmol), tetra-n-butylammonium bromide (TBAB) (13.0 mg, 0.04 mmol), and Pd(PPh3)4 (56.0 mg, 0.048 mmol). The mixture was degassed by bubbling with nitrogen for 30 min and then heated at 120 °C for 72 h. After cooling to room temperature, bromobenzene was added and then the sealed tube was heated at 120 °C for 6 h, followed by the addition of phenylboronic acid and heating at 120 °C for another 6 h. The mixture was cooled to room temperature and poured into MeOH. The precipitate was collected through membrane filtration. The purification of the polymer was performed through Soxhlet extraction with MeOH and hexane. Finally, the polymer was dissolved in hot CHCl3, concentrated, then precipitated in MeOH. The polymer was collected and dried under a vacuum. GPC (THF): M w 51.5 kg mol–1; 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3): δ 8.35 (br), 7.74 (br), 7.68 (br), 7.52 (br), 4.86 (br), 2.25 (br), 2.08 (br), 1.44 (br), 1.35−1.23 (m), 1.18−1.09 (m), 0.87 (br), 0.78 (br), 0.42 (br).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Both wide-bandgap NCQD (wNCQDs) and small-bandgap NCQD (sNCQDs) were synthesized by the solvothermal method from Citric Acid with diaminonaphthalene [33].

CA (20 mg) and 2,3-diaminonaphthalene (20 mg) were dissolved in ethanol (10 mL), the solution was then put in Teflon lined hydrothermal autoclave reactors (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 9 h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally, then the solution will centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 1 h to remove any aggregated particles. Finally, the solutions undergo dialysis for two days using the 0.3 K Dalton dialysis membrane to obtain purified NCQDs. 13C MAS NMR (100 MHz): δ 238.5 (br), 172.6 (br), 154.9 (br), 143.8 (br), 127.3 (br), 112.4 (br), 109.0 (br), 61.0 (br), 14.7 (br). 15N MAS NMR (40.48 MHz): δ 148.4 (br). Elemental analysis found C 73.11 %, N 11.98 %, O 10.55 %, H 5.44 %.

CA (10 mg) and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (20 mg) were dissolved in ethanol (10 mL), the solution was then put in the reactor (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 9 h. Then following the same procedure for wNCQDs. 13C MAS NMR (100 MHz): δ 235.0 (br), 177.3 (br), 171.1 (br), 144.1 (br), 124.9 (br), 110.8 (br), 73.6 (br), 61.7 (br), 42.9 (br), 14.5 (br). 15N MAS NMR (40.48 MHz): δ 53.2 (br). Elemental analysis found C 69.82 %, N 11.57 %, O 13.22 %, H 5.22 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCQDs/PFTBTA pdots heterostructured photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, a polymer solution (1.0 mg mL–1 in THF, 600 μL), NCQDs solution (0.1 mg mL–1 in THF, 60 μL in case of 1:100 w/w ratio from NCQDs and polymer) and PS-PEG-COOH (1.0 mg mL–1 in THF, 300 μL) were mixed with THF (5 mL). This solution was rapidly added into water (10 mL) under sonication. Then the solution was purged with N2 (slow rate) on a hot plate at 100 °C for 90 min to remove the THF. To separate any agglomerated particles formed during the preparation process, the Pdot solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm cellulose membrane filter. To prepare different ratios of NCQDs and polymer (0.1:100, 0.5:100, 1:100, 2:100 and 4:100 w/w) different volumes from NCQDs were added (6, 30, 60, 120 and 240 μL respectively, 0.1 mg mL–1 in THF). For the preparation of the PFTBTA/PS-PEG-COOH (PFTBTA Pdot) or NCQDs/PS-PEG-COOH, the same procedures are followed with a polymer solution only (1 mg mL–1 in THF, 600 μL) or NCQDs solution only (1.0 mg mL–1 in THF, 600 μL), respectively (As shown in Fig. 2 ).

1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded in solution at 500 MHz, using a Bruker Avance 500 MHz NMR spectrometer. 13C NMR spectra of the NCQDs were acquired using 4 mm double resonance probes with a spinning rate of 11 kHz. 15N NMR spectra of the NCQDs were acquired using 4 mm double resonance probes with a spinning rate of 10 kHz. The molecular weights of the polymers were measured on a Hitachi Gel Permeation Chromatography and 5450 RI detector with polystyrene standards; the mobile phase was tetrahydrofuran (flow rate: 1 mL min–1) at 40 °C. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the polymers was recorded under N2 using a TA Q600 instrument over the temperature range 20–600 °C (heating rate: 10 °C min–1). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected using a ULVAC-PHI PHI 5000 Versaprobe II chemical analysis electron spectrometer (ESCA). The materials thin films were prepared from drop-casting on ITO substrates for XPS measurement. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) measurements were performed on a Bruker instruments D2-Phaser. Imaging of the Pdot morphologies was obtained using a JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, the samples were prepared using drop-casting sonicated water suspension of the Pdots onto a copper grid. The hydrodynamic diameter of the Pdots was measured on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Nordic AB). UV–vis absorption spectra of the materials were performed on Hitachi U-3310 spectrophotometers. Fluorescence spectra were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer at room temperature. Optical bandgaps (E g) were calculated from the onsets of the absorption spectra. Where, it can be determined directly from absorption spectrum, which is related to electron excitation from the valence band to conduction band. Extrapolating the straight-line portion to wavelength axis at zero absorption give the direct optical band gaps through the following equation:

The energy levels of the LUMOs were calculated by subtracting the E g from the HOMO energy levels. The energy levels of the HOMOs were recorded for PFTBTA polymer by a photoelectron spectrometer (model AC-2) (Fig. S1d) or cyclic voltammetry (CV) (Zahner Zennium E) for wNCQDs (Fig. S1a) and sNCQDs (Fig. S1b). For cyclic voltammetry, the tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6) (0.1 M) was used as a supporting electrolyte dissolved in acetonitrile. The conventional three-electrode configuration consists of a platinum working electrode, a platinum wire auxiliary electrode, and an Ag/AgNO3 wire pseudo-reference electrode with ferrocenium-ferrocene (Fc+/Fc) as the internal standard, then the HOMO value calculated from this equation [40].
EHOMO = – [(Eox onset - E1/2 (ferrocene) + 4.8]

Time-resolved transient photoluminescence decay spectra of the materials were performed using a spectrometer (FLS980, Edinburgh Instruments) with a gated photomultiplier tube. Photocurrent measurements were performed on a Zahner Zennium E workstation equipped with a three-electrode cell including a Pt wire counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode (3 M NaCl) and a fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass as the working electrode. About 2 mL of Pdot solution (2 mg/mL) containing 30 μL of Nafion (5 wt%) and sonicate for 10 min. After that, 30 μL of as-prepared suspension was dropped on FTO glass with an active area of 0.5 cm2. Here, 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was prepared as an electrolyte. 1.1 V constant potential was applied with the 40 s light on-off after a certain time interval to record the photo and dark current under LED light irradiation.

Time-resolved experiments were carried out on laser-based spectroscopy, with laser powers equating to less than one photon absorption per particle. Samples for transient absorption experiments were kept in dark between each measurement. A Coherent Legend Ti: Sapphire amplifier (800 nm,100 fs pulse length, 3 kHz repetition rate) was used. The output is split to pump and probe beams. Excitation pulses at the wavelength of 450 nm were acquired using an optical parametric amplifier (Topas C, Light Conversion). The probe pulses (a broad supercontinuum spectrum) were generated from the 800-nm pulses in a CaF2 crystal and split by a beam splitter into a probe pulse and a reference pulse. The probe pulse and the reference pulse were dispersed in a spectrograph and detected by a diode array. Instrumental response time is ∼ 100 fs.

In a typical measurement, the Pdot solution (8.0 mL) containing DEA (2.0 mL) was inserted into the reaction glass container and sealed tightly with a septum. The resulting mixture was degassed by Ar bubbling, prior to illumination. A white light-emitting diode (LED) PAR38 lamp (20 W, 6500 K, Zenaro Lighting; λ > 420 nm) was used as the light source. Hydrogen samples were taken with a gas-tight syringe and injected in a Shimazhu GC-2014 gas chromatograph, with Ar as the carrier gas. Hydrogen was detected with a thermal conductivity detector, referring to the standard hydrogen gases with known concentrations. Increased pressure from the evolved hydrogen is neglected in the calculations. Water for the H2 evolution experiments was purified using an ELGA LabWater system. The apparent quantum yields (AQYs) for the photocatalytic H2 evolution were determined using the KIT-XENON-ADJ350W light source with a band-pass filter at a central wavelength of 420 nm for wNCQDs/PFTBTA-Pdots. Then, the AQY was calculated using the following equation [41]:

Where, C is the amount of H2 evolution per hour; NA is Avogadro constant; S is the irradiation area; P is the incident monochromatic light intensity; t is the light irradiation time; λ is the monochromatic light wavelength; h is Plank constant; c is vacuum light velocity.

In our previous work, we designed the molecule poly-(5-fluoreneothiophen-2-yl)-5,6-difluoro-2-octyl-2H-benzo[d][1,2,3]triazole (PFTBTA) as a conjugated polymer photocatalyst because we believed that PFTBTA are potential photocatalysts for visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution [31]. The as-prepared polymers of all related properties including those obtained by gel permeation chromatography (GPC), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) are summarized in Table S1 and Fig. S2. The percentage of Pd residue (0.0007 wt%) measured by ICP-MS indicated the prepared PFTBTA polymer is a proximately metal-free photocatalyst (Table S1). Most importantly, the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence measurements demonstrated the difference between these two NCQDs, as shown in Figs. S3a and b. The E g was determined from their absorption onset to be 2.53 eV and 1.85 eV for the wNCQDs and sNCQDs, respectively (Table 1 ). The morphology and structure of these NCQDs were determined by TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS) spectroscopy and are shown in Fig. 3 a, b. The TEM images revealed that the NCQDs have spherical particles with average diameters of 4 nm and 18 nm for wNCQDs and sNCQDs, respectively. The DLS spectra revealed that the hydrodynamic diameter of both NCQDs are consistent with the TEM results. The surface chemical states, composition, and oxidation states of the NCQDs were determined using XPS. The XPS analysis spectra in Fig. S3c show the C 1s (288.4), O 1s (531.6), and N 1s (399.5) peaks of the NCQDs (Table S2). The percentages of N-doped wNCQDs and sNCQDs are approximately 4.21 % and 7.7 %, respectively. In addition, the high resolution of the O 1s spectra is shown in Fig. S3d and f for both samples. One peak at 531.4 eV could be related to the oxygen in the CO group, and no other peak related to the oxygen in the OH group appeared [37]. Fig. S3e presented the FTIR for both wNCQDs and sNCQDs, which shows the bands related to CO, CC, and CN groups. has a small peak at 3300 cm−1, indicating the presence of the OH group. The Raman spectra (Fig. 3c) contain two peaks at 1357 and 1578 cm−1, which are attributed to the disordered D and crystalline G bands of carbon materials, respectively. The disordered D bands are much lower than the crystalline G bands, indicating the high quality and crystallinity of the graphene structure of the NCQDs. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the NCQDs are shown in Fig. 3d, and the broad (002) peaks located at approximately 25° proved the graphitic structure of both NCQD samples. 13C NMR of wNCQDs and sNCQDs shows a clear band of the aromatic carbons, indicating the graphitic structure of the prepared materials. From the elemental analysis, the percentages of N content for both wNCQDs and sNCQDs are almost identical. However, 15N NMR shows different positions of N elements inside the chemical structure of wNCQDs and sNCQDs (Figs. S4–S7).

The sessile drop technique was used to measure the static contact angle of the prepared PFTBTA, wNCQDs and sNCQDs, and the contact angles were measured at three different locations with water at room temperature. The water contact angles for PFTBTA, wNCQDs and sNCQDs are 112.4°, 101.4° and 97.9° (Fig. 4 ), respectively. In addition, all the NCQDs and PFTBTA are soluble in common organic solvents such as tetrahydrofuran (THF) and ethanol but aggregate together in an aqueous solution (Fig. S8a–c). These results indicated that PFTBTA, wNCQDs, and sNCQDs are hydrophobic materials.

To confirm the implantation of the wNCQDs inside the PFTBTA Pdot particle during the preparation of wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot, we performed the same preparation procedures with wNCQDs and PFTBTA alone for comparison following the above procedures (Fig. 2). Notably, the resulting solutions in the case of both wNCQD/PS-PEGCOOH and PFTBTA/PS-PEGCOOH (PFTBTA Pdots) are clear without turbidity (Fig. 2), indicating the high dispersity of wNCQD/PS-PEGCOOH and PFTBTA Pdots separately in water. In addition, the prepared wNCQD/PS-PEGCOOH and PFTBTA Pdots using PS-PEGCOOH decreased the water contact angle to 54.1° and 39.2°. TEM and DLS results revealed that the wNCQD/PS-PEGCOOH and PFTBTA Pdot particles are spherical-like shapes with average hydrodynamic diameters of 59.6 and 21.3 nm, respectively (Fig. 4). All of the results indicate that the wNCQDs have identical behaviour as PFTBTA while using PS-PEGCOOH, and in the case of using a mixture of PFTBTA and wNCQDs together with PS-PEGCOOH, PS-PEGCOOH acts as a surfactant to form water-miscible micelles that contain the hydrophobic wNCQDs and PFTBTA inside. Similarly, the as-prepared wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots were characterized by TEM, and DLS revealed that the hydrodynamic diameter of these Pdots is in the range of 16–68 nm with an average particle size of 27.7 nm (Figs. 4 and S9). Fig. S9d shows a HRTEM image of wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot, showing the lattice spacing is 0.23 nm, which agrees well with that of pure wNCQDs (Fig. S9b) and related to the in-plane (100) lattice spacing of graphitic structure. This indicates the presence of wNCQDs inside the PFTBTA Pdot [42]. The water contact angle of wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots is 45.8°, indicating good contact between the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots and water (Fig. 4).

XPS analysis was performed to examine the surface chemical composition of C, O, S, F and N in the wNCQDs, PFTBTA Pdots and wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots (Fig. 5 a). Notably, no other elements were observed in the XPS spectra, which indicated no impurity is produced during the preparation process of the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots. Fig. 5c shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s for the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots and PFTBTA Pdots. For the PFTBTA Pdots, there are three peaks at 283.1, 283.9 and 285 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbon bonding (sp2 C), sp3 carbon bonding (sp3 C), and the carbon in CN bonding, respectively [43,44]. All carbon peaks of PFTBTA Pdot shifted to higher binding energies (0.5 eV for sp2 and sp3, 0.3 eV for CN) after combining the wNCQDs and PFTBTA in the Pdot structure, indicating that the electrons from PFTBTA transferred into the wNCQDs. Then, the electron density around the atoms in PFTBTA decreased, leading to an increase in the binding energy [45]. A new small peak appeared after the combination of wNCQDs and PFTBTA at 287.9 eV (as shown with magnification), which is related to the carbon atom in the NCN or CO groups from wNCQDs, as shown in the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of the NCQDs (Fig. 5b) [27]. Likewise, the S 2p spectrum for PFTBTA Pdots can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 162.3 and 163.4 eV, corresponding to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene, respectively (Fig. S10a), which shifted to higher binding energy (0.3 eV) after combination with the wNCQDs [28]. Fig. 5d shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s for the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots and wNCQDs. The N 1s spectra of the wNCQD sample can be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 397.6, 399.2 and 400.8 eV, corresponding to the nitrogen atoms in CNC (pyridinic N), CNH (amino group) and N(C)3 groups, respectively [28]. After the wNCQDs are combined with PFTBTA, the two peaks related to the nitrogen atoms in the CNC and CNH groups of wNCQDs shifted to lower binding energies (approximately 0.4 and 0.6 eV, respectively), indicating that the electrons from PFTBTA transferred to the wNCQDs and that the electron density around the atoms in the wNCQDs increased, leading to a decrease in the binding energy. The nitrogen atom in the N(C)3 group of wNCQDs barely shifted for the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots, which indicated a weak interaction between the nitrogen atom in the N(C)3 group and PFTBTA. Similarly, the O 1s spectrum of the wNCQD sample had a peak centered at 531.2 eV related to the oxygen in the CO group, which shifted to a lower binding energy (approximately 0.4 eV) for the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots (Fig. S10b). The shift of the binding energy peaks confirmed that our design of the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots by the space confinement effect in nanometer-sized Pdots can truly enhance the interaction between the materials compared to the normal blending between the two materials. Where the XPS data for the normal blending between PFTBTA polymer and wNCQDs showed a small shift of binding energy for the N1 s peak (Fig. S11) [46,47].

The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the PFTBTA Pdots and wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots were measured to evaluate the optical properties before and after adding the wNCQDS. As presented in Fig. 6 a, the PFTBTA Pdots exhibit the typical absorption edge at approximately 550 nm, which is suitable for absorbing visible light. The PL peak of the PFTBTA Pdots is centered at 591 nm, and remarkably, the overlap between the absorption and emission spectra of the PFTBTA Pdots is quite small, leading to minimal self-absorption. The obtained UV–vis spectra is the same for both the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots and PFTBTA Pdots. As the concentration of wNCQDs was increased larger than 1 %, the change of the absorption spectra only presented in the UV region (300–350 nm) and there was no change of the absorption of PFTBTA at the visible-light region (Fig. 6b), suggesting that the optical absorption after adding the wNCQDs to PFTBTA was not affected the catalytic efficiencies significantly.

Next, we examined the wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots as metal-free photocatalysts for visible-light–driven H2 evolution for 6 h with irradiation from an LED light source (20 W, 6500 K, λ > 420 nm, unless otherwise stated) under ambient conditions. The photocatalytic systems were assembled by adding sacrificial electron donors (diethylamine (DEA), 20 %), to a Pdot aqueous solution in a glass photoreactor (50 mL). We simplified our photocatalytic system consist of water with DEA only, instead of the three components (water/DEA/methanol) which used the methanol as the co-solvent and as the electron donor sacrificial reagent. Therefore, our system is no need to concern about where the methanol might be oxidized photocatalytically to aldehydes, carboxylic acid and CO2 as side products as well as hydrogen [48]. The H2 gas evolved was periodically monitored by gas chromatography (GC). Fig. 7 a and b shows the H2 evolution rate of the PFTBTA Pdots with the two different types of NCQDs (wNCQDs and sNCQDs). The amount of generated H2 reached a maximum value of 10,750 μmol g–1 after 6 h with 1 % wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots, which was almost five times higher than that obtained with pure PFTBTA Pdots (2400 μmol g–1) (Fig. 7a). In contrast, the addition of sNCQDs into PFTBTA Pdots inhibited the photocatalytic efficiency of the PFTBTA Pdots, as shown in Fig. 7b, where the amount of generated H2 after 6 h decreased from 2400 μmol g−1 with PFTBTA Pdots to 1400 μmol g−1 with sNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots. We attribute this reduction to the large particle size of the sNCQDs (average 18 nm), which is not suitable for insertion inside the PFTBTA Pdots (average particle size 21.3 nm), and have different optical properties (bandgap, HOMO/LUMO). We optimized the catalyst composition by measuring the HER for different mass percentages of NCQDs with a fixed mass of the polymer. Fig. S12a shows the HER of 0.5 %, 1 %, 2 %, and 4 % of wNCQDs implanted inside PFTBTA Pdots, where all used concentrations of wNCQDs are enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency and the 1 % wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot is the optimized one. Fig. S12b shows the optimized concentrations of sNCQDs with PFTBTA Pdot. Increasing the wNCQDs concentration increased the HER rate to a maximum value of 2400 μmol g–1 h–1 with 1 % wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots, which is approximately 6 times higher than that of the pure PFTBTA Pdots (440 μmol g–1 h–1) (Fig. S13). In Table S3, we summarize the HER performances of the reported photocatalysts with and without the use of Pt cocatalysts, and among the results, our design and methods to achieve the highest photocatalytic efficiencies were under metal-free solution conditions. To investigate the role of PS-PEGCOOH polymer as a nano-cage or as a surfactant, we compared between the nanometer-sized Pdots structure (with PS-PEGCOOH) and the physical blending (without PS-PEGCOOH). The physical blending between wNCQDs and PFTBTA without PS-PEGCOOH produce aggregated and precipitated particles of wNCQDs + PFTBTA in the aqueous solution with an average particles diameter >2 μm (as shown in Fig. S14a and b). While by following our design structure for the combination between wNCQDs and PFTBTA using the PS-PEGCOOH as a surfactant produces a clear solution of the nanometer-sized particles (wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot) dispersed well in the aqueous solution with average particles diameter of 27.7 nm (as shown in Fig. S14a and d). In the absence of PS-PEGCOOH the physical blending of wNCQDs and PFTBTA showed lower hydrogen evolution efficiency than those of the wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot structure (in presence of PS-PEGCOOH), as shown in Fig. S14c. This result was strongly indicated the importance of our design structure for the combination between the NCQDs and PFTBTA as the photocatalyst. The apparent quantum yield (AQY) of the most efficient 1 % wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot photocatalyst was also measured at 420 nm, providing an AQY value of 0.56 %. The addition of wNCQDs to the Pdot structure not only enhanced the HER of the Pdots but also increased the stability of the hydrogen evolution rate. The amount of hydrogen produced from 1 % wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdots still increased with a high rate of 20 h. However, the PFTBTA Pdots produce H2 until 8 h only (Fig. 7c). Photos in Fig. 7c present the colour changing of the PFTBTA Pdots solution before and after the H2 evolution experiment, and the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 7d) show that the mean absorption band of the polymer in the visible region (at 472 nm) disappeared, while another band appeared at 388 nm, which explains the inability of the PFTBTA Pdots to continue producing hydrogen for a long time. However, regarding 1 % wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdots, the colour is stable, and the absorption band at 472 nm still remains, although the second band at 388 nm arises (Fig. 7d). Based on the previous studies and our results we are suggesting that [49,50], during the photoexcitation of pure PFTBTA Pdot photocatalyst, the excited electrons were accumulated on the HOMO of PFTBTA, these excited electrons lead to the photodegradation of the PFTBTA polymer structure. As a result of this photodegradation, the mean UV–vis absorption band of PFTBTA polymer was blue-shifted, and the H2 evolution rate was decreased with the long time. While after the photoexcitation of wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot, the accumulated electrons on the HOMO of PFTBTA would first transfer to the wNCQDs which have a large electron-storage capacity and act as an electron mediator, then the photodegradation of the PFTBTA was inhibited, the mean UV–vis absorption band of PFTBTA polymer remain, and the H2 evolution rate become approximately stable with the long time (reach to 20 h). The replacement of DEA (sacrificial electron donor) with ascorbic acid (AA) enhances the HER of 1 % wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdots from 2400 to 4490 μmol g–1 h–1, as shown in Fig. S15. The reusability of wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot for H2 production was shown in Fig. S16, the wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot exhibited a good photocatalytic stability for 4 cycles. This was contributed by the introduction of NCQDs to the PFTBTA Pdots. However, it showed a decrease of the performance in the fifth cycle, we considered that because of the degradation of the polymer which was also observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot before and after photocatalytic hydrogen reaction. To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdots for light-driven hydrogen evolution, we performed the H2 evolution reaction one time in the absence of DEA as a sacrificial electron donor and another time in the absence of the photocatalyst (Fig. 8 a). The results show that the absence of any component (photocatalyst, DEA or light) results in no activity under otherwise identical conditions, confirming the photocatalytic process of this system.

To determine the role of the wNCQDs with PFTBTA during the photocatalytic reaction, we measured the time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectra. The lifetimes obtained when the PFTBTA Pdots contained wNCQDs (0.84 ns for 1 % wNCQD/PFTBTA Pdots) were shorter than that of the pure PFTBTA Pdot system (1.05 ns) (Fig. 8b) [17]. In addition, Fig. 8c shows the photoresponse ability of the corresponding samples using the transient photocurrent response which was collected under visible-light irradiation at 1.1 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). The photocurrent-time (I-t) curves for samples were adopted by switch on-off illumination method. The result indicates that the combination between wNCQDs and PFTBTA Pdot enhanced the photocurrent response, where wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot presented a higher and stable current density (5.0 μA) compared to those of PFTBTA Pdot (2.5 μA). This evidence that the wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot has high and good sensitivity to the light illumination, which corresponding to the result of photocatalytic HER. Moreover, the photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 8d) obtained from PFTBTA Pdot strongly quenched after inserting the wNCQDs to PFTBTA Pdot. This indicates that the recombination of electron-hole pairs in wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot significantly suppressed compared to that of PFTBTA Pdot because of enhancing the charge transfer process during the photocatalytic reaction after adding the wNCQDs.

To fundamentally understand the photophysics behind the catalytic activity of our system, we measure the fs transient absorption spectra of neat PFTBTA Pdot, wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot, and pure wNCQDs. The transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) investigate the excited state dynamics of the PFTBTA polymer and how these dynamics changed in the presence of wNCQDs. Fig. 9 a shows the TA spectra at different delay times of wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot (in water solution) after 450 nm excitation pulse. Immediately after excitation, the transient spectra for PFTBTA Pdot consist of a negative signal in the 450–550 nm range and an excited state absorption above 600 nm. The negative absorption peak can be assigned to the ground state bleach of the polymer consistent with the ground state absorption band in Fig. 6a, while the positive absorption peak above 600 nm assigned to the absorption of the polymers’ excitonic state, which had been reported for similar polymers that contain fluorene backbone [51,52]. In the neat PFTBTA Pdot, an additional short-lived negative signal between 550 and 600 nm overlaps spectrally with the polymer’s ground state bleach and it is located at the steady-state emission band (Fig. 9b), therefore we assigned it to stimulated emission of the polymer [15]. This stimulated emission peak disappeared in the wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot system (Fig. 9a) which indicates the electronic interaction between the excited PFTBTA Pdot and wNCQDs.

On one hand, the TA spectra of PFTBTA Pdot (Fig. 9b) and wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot (Fig. 9a) systems look similar, which confirms that inserting wNCQDs in PFTBTA Pdot does not affect the optical properties of the PFTBTA Pdot. Indeed, no signal appears for pure wNCQDs (Fig. 9c) at 450 nm excitation wavelength which is blow its absorption band edge (Fig. S3a). On the other hand, both systems (PFTBTA Pdot and wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot) are different. Inserting the wNCQDs inside the PFTBTA Pdot structure slowdown the ground-state bleach (GSB) recovery. Here, the bleach recovery dynamics at 477 nm consist of three-time components for neat PFTBTA Pdot (Fig. 9d - Redline) (τ1 = 2 ps, τ2 = 30 ps, and τ3 = 920 ps), and for wNCQDs/PFTBTA Pdot (Green line) (τ1 = 3 ps, τ2 = 55 ps, and τ3 = 1.5 ns). For both systems with and without the wNCQDs the very fast time component can be attributed to non-geminate recombination that reported elsewhere [51] and shows a clear power-dependent (Fig. S17). Clear evidence that inserting wNCQDs decelerated the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers. In other words, for neat PFTBTA Pdot the electrons recombine through two-time components (τ2 = 30 ps, τ3 = 920 ps). While, the dynamics suggest that the recombination becomes almost two times longer in the presence of wNCQDs inside PFTBTA Pdot (τ2 = 55 ps, τ3 = 1.5 ns). We assume that the wNCQDs can work as an electron mediator that leads to slowdown the GSB recovery and increase the lifetime of excited electrons. This increase in the excited state lifetime by introducing wNCQDs in the full system (in the presence of sacrificial electron donor) is the main reason behind the catalytic activity enhancement (Fig. 10 ).

We have successfully developed a metal-free photocatalytic H2 production system consisting of NCQDs and PFTBTA as photocatalysts implanted inside Pdots for the first time under visible-light-driven and metal-free conditions. We believe that this work may provide new insights into the development of non-metal and inexpensive NCQD-based Pdot photocatalysts with high solar energy conversion efficiency in artificial photosynthetic systems. Their photophysical properties and photocatalytic performance have been characterized and compared to those of conjugated polymers. The introduction of N-doped CQDs into PFTBTA Pdots results in a dramatic enhancement in their photocatalytic performance and stability. As expected that such low-cost and highly efficient heterojunction Pdot systems will have promising applications in the generation of environmentally friendly and renewable energy.
